behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS/MOCK-HIDE: a unit test of the HMAC function can pass while real handler wiring fails (raw-body bytes, header parsing, middleware ordering, or accidental enqueue call). REAL-INFRA: no OS/external dependency required beyond local app + queue adapter.
behaviors.test_description: Run the HTTP handler stack with real raw-body parsing and HMAC verification, send a request with a tampered body/signature pair, assert `401` and assert no queue write occurred.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-HIDE: mocking queue writes can hide ordering bugs. Need real persistence path to verify ack happens only after durable enqueue success. No external network/subprocess required if queue backend is local DB/filesystem.
behaviors.test_description: Use the real persistent queue backend (test DB/file). Instrument enqueue completion and response send time; assert enqueue commit completes before `200` is observed.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: unit tests may miss accidental `await` on processor path. Need wired handler + async worker boundary to prove response decoupling. REAL-INFRA system-level not strictly required.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler to a downstream processor that intentionally sleeps (e.g., 10s). Send valid webhook, assert immediate HTTP success while processor is still running, then assert processor finishes later.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: this is a wall-clock SLA under concurrency; scheduler, socket handling, and process-level contention can cause failures that in-process mocks miss. MOCK-HIDE: mocked timing/concurrency often always passes.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service as a real process, create concurrent load with earlier events tied to slow processing, then send a fresh valid webhook and measure end-to-end client-observed latency; assert response < 3s.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-HIDE: dedupe correctness depends on persistence semantics (unique key/transaction/race handling), which mocks can miss. REAL-INFRA: local DB/filesystem queue is sufficient; no external API needed.
behaviors.test_description: Using the real queue store, submit the same event ID twice (including a concurrent double-submit case) and assert only one persisted queue record exists.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Need real error mapping between queue layer and HTTP layer; pure unit mocks can miss actual queue-full signal shape/codes and header behavior. System-level infra not required if capacity can be reached locally.
behaviors.test_description: Fill the real queue to capacity, send another valid webhook, assert HTTP `503` and presence/value format of `Retry-After`, and assert event not enqueued.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS/MOCK-HIDE: capacity enforcement is in queue persistence logic; mocks of queue API can trivially pass. Needs real queue backend behavior with configured limit.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the persistent queue with a small configured capacity, enqueue up to limit, attempt one more enqueue, assert explicit rejection and unchanged persisted depth.
